title: Witch Week - Chapter 16 (Fragment 1)
text:
16

16

“I ’M VERY SORRY, SIR,” said one of the boys. He sounded as if he meant it, for a wonder.

Mr. Crossley jumped, and wondered if he had been asleep. He seemed to have had that kind of shiver that makes you say, “Someone walked over my grave.” He looked up from the books he was marking.

The janitor was in the classroom. What was his name? He had a raucous voice and a lot of stupid opinions. Littleton, that was it. Littleton seemed to be clearing up broken glass. Mr. Crossley was puzzled, because he did not remember a window being broken. But when he looked over at the windows, he saw one of them was newly mended, with a lot of putty and many thumbprints.

“There you are, Mr. Crossley. All tidy now,” Mr. Littleton rasped.

“Thank you, Littleton,” Mr. Crossley said coldly. If you let Littleton get talking, he stayed and tried to teach the class. He watched the janitor collect his things and back himself through the door. Thank goodness!

“Thank you, Charles,” said someone.

Mr. Crossley jerked around and discovered a total stranger in the room. This man was tall and tired-looking and seemed, from his clothes, to be on his way to a wedding. Mr. Crossley thought he must be a school governor and started to stand up politely.

“Oh, please don’t get up,” Chrestomanci said. “I’m just on my way out.” He walked to the door. Before he went out of it, he looked around 6B and said, “If any of you want me again, a message to the Old Gate House should find me.”

The door closed behind him. Mr. Crossley sat down to his marking again. He stared. There was a note on top of the topmost exercise book. He knew it had not been there before. It was written in ordinary blue ballpoint, in capital letters, and it gave Mr. Crossley the oddest feeling of having been in the same situation before. Why was that? He must have dozed off and had a dream. Yes. Now Mr. Crossley thought about it, he had had the strangest dream. He had dreamed he taught in a dreadful boarding school called Larwood House. He looked thankfully up at the bent, busy heads of 6B. This was, as he well knew, Portway Oaks Comprehensive, and everyone went home every evening. Thank goodness! Mr. Crossley hated the idea of teaching in a boarding school. You were always on the job.

He wondered who had written the note. And here, as his eyes went over the class, he had a momentary feeling of shock. A lot of faces were missing from his dream. He remembered a batch of tiresome girls: Theresa Mullett, Delia Martin, Heather Something, Karen Something Else. None of them was there. Nor was Daniel Smith.

Ah but—Mr. Crossley remembered now. Dan Smith should have been there. He was in the hospital. Two days ago, the stupid boy had eaten a handful of tintacks for a bet. No one had believed he had, at first. But when Mr. Wentworth, the headmaster, had put Dan in his car and driven him to be X-rayed, there he was, full of tintacks. Idiots some boys were!

vocabulary.definition: A pen with a tiny ball bearing that dispenses ink as it moves across paper.
vocabulary.example: It was written in ordinary blue ballpoint, in capital letters, and it gave Mr.

vocabulary.definition: A quantity or group of things produced or considered at one time.
vocabulary.example: He remembered a batch of tiresome girls: Theresa Mullett, Delia Martin, Heather Something, Karen Something Else.

vocabulary.definition: An agreement in which someone risks money on an uncertain event.
vocabulary.example: Two days ago, the stupid boy had eaten a handful of tintacks for a bet.

vocabulary.definition: A school where students live during the term time.
vocabulary.example: Crossley thought he must be a school governor and started to stand up politely.

vocabulary.definition: Letters of the alphabet that are written or printed in uppercase form.
vocabulary.example: It was written in ordinary blue ballpoint, in capital letters, and it gave Mr.

vocabulary.definition: A fictional title/name representing a powerful enchanter or official in a magical setting.
vocabulary.example: “Oh, please don’t get up,” Chrestomanci said.

vocabulary.definition: In an unemotional or unfriendly manner.
vocabulary.example: Crossley said coldly.

vocabulary.definition: Including all or nearly all elements or aspects of something.
vocabulary.example: This was, as he well knew, Portway Oaks Comprehensive, and everyone went home every evening.

vocabulary.definition: Fell asleep lightly or briefly.
vocabulary.example: He must have dozed off and had a dream.

vocabulary.definition: Causing fear, shock, or distress; very bad or unpleasant.
vocabulary.example: He had dreamed he taught in a dreadful boarding school called Larwood House.

vocabulary.definition: A notebook used for writing school exercises.
vocabulary.example: ” He looked up from the books he was marking.

vocabulary.definition: A person with authority to oversee or manage an institution, such as a school.
vocabulary.example: Crossley thought he must be a school governor and started to stand up politely.

vocabulary.definition: People who show very poor judgment or intelligence.
vocabulary.example: Idiots some boys were!

vocabulary.definition: To turn or move abruptly.
vocabulary.example: Crossley jerked around and discovered a total stranger in the room.

vocabulary.definition: Repaired or fixed.
vocabulary.example: But when he looked over at the windows, he saw one of them was newly mended, with a lot of putty and many thumbprints.

vocabulary.definition: Lasting for a very short time.
vocabulary.example: And here, as his eyes went over the class, he had a momentary feeling of shock.

vocabulary.definition: Confused or perplexed.
vocabulary.example: Crossley was puzzled, because he did not remember a window being broken.

vocabulary.definition: Spoke or said in a rough, harsh-sounding voice.
vocabulary.example: Littleton rasped.

vocabulary.definition: Making a disturbingly harsh and loud noise.
vocabulary.example: He had a raucous voice and a lot of stupid opinions.

vocabulary.definition: A sudden upsetting or surprising event or experience.
vocabulary.example: And here, as his eyes went over the class, he had a momentary feeling of shock.

vocabulary.definition: Having or showing a lack of intelligence or common sense.
vocabulary.example: He had a raucous voice and a lot of stupid opinions.

vocabulary.definition: Expressing gratitude or relief.
vocabulary.example: He looked thankfully up at the bent, busy heads of 6B.

vocabulary.definition: An impression or mark made by a thumb, especially as a form of identification.
vocabulary.example: But when he looked over at the windows, he saw one of them was newly mended, with a lot of putty and many thumbprints.

vocabulary.definition: Neatly arranged and in order.
vocabulary.example: All tidy now,” Mr.

vocabulary.definition: Small pointed pins used for fastening papers to surfaces.
vocabulary.example: Two days ago, the stupid boy had eaten a handful of tintacks for a bet.

vocabulary.definition: Appearing to be tired.
vocabulary.example: This man was tall and tired-looking and seemed, from his clothes, to be on his way to a wedding.

vocabulary.definition: Examined by means of X-rays.
vocabulary.example: Wentworth, the headmaster, had put Dan in his car and driven him to be X-rayed, there he was, full of tintacks.
